attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner complaining of faecal soiling. This first occurred a month ago when she was at a shopping centre. There was a significant amount of faecal matter, which she states was very embarrassing for her. After that it has happened on five further occasions, all at random times and unpredictable. She feels the urge to go but often too late. She denies constipation and diarrhoea but describes her stool as normally between type 5 and 6 on the Bristol stool chart.

She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes on insulin, G3P3 with a history of grade 4 tear post instrumental vaginal delivery with Neville Barnes forceps. On examination she has reduced resting anal tone and decreased sphincter squeeze pressure.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Disimpaction with enemas with encouragement to increase fibre intake
Take stool sample and commence metronidazole to treat for Clostridioides difficile
Commence bulk forming agent in combination with biofeedback therapy
Referral to specialist continence clinic for sacral nerve stimulation
Commence antidiarrheal agent such as loperamide and recommend avoiding caffeine, spicy foods, dairy and fatty foods.
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation is depicting a picture of faecal incontinence of a neuromuscular dysfunction origin:

  • Urge to pass stool present but have passive loss of stool

  • Digital rectal examination finding often shows weak resting and squeezing pressures with evidence of leakage

  • Risk factors - prior O&G injury, diabetes, neuropathy, CNS/spinal disease, sphincter disruption.

The absence of constipation and Bristol stool chart type 5 & 6 stool consistency renders disimpaction unnecessary as a management option for this scenario. Lack of diarrhoea and abdominal symptoms make the likelihood of Clostridioides difficile infection unlikely as well. With the underlying aetiology likely from a combination of previous grade 4 tear in the perineal region impacting the sphincter and diabetes impacting the neurotransmission, the use of antidiarrhoeal medication will have limited role in reducing incontinence. The most appropriate treatment to commence is to recommend avoiding caffeine, spicy foods, dairy and fatty foods and trial bulking agents with biofeedback concurrently. Possibly, next option to explore if the bulking agent and biofeedback fails is to explore more invasive measures such as sacral nerve stimulation, surgery or colostomy, however, they will not be the most appropriate "next-step" management.
learningPoints: Previous obstetric injury can predispose to future faecal incontinence.
Biofeedback therapy combined with bulk-forming agents is an effective initial management strategy for faecal incontinence due to neuromuscular dysfunction.
references: Continence Foundation of Australia. Faecal incontinence. 2024. [Internet]. Accessed 12 Jan 2025. Available from: https://www.continence.org.au/types-incontinence/faecal-incontinence.

Turel FD, Langer S, Shek KL, Dietz HP. Medium- to Long-term Follow-up of Obstetric Anal Sphincter Injury. Dis Colon Rectum. 2019 Mar;62(3):348-356. doi: 10.1097/DCR.0000000000001297. PMID: 30543535. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30543535/

Brown HW, Dyer KY, Rogers RG. Management of Fecal Incontinence. Obstet Gynecol. 2020 Oct;136(4):811-822. doi: 10.1097/AOG.0000000000004054. PMID: 32925633. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32925633/

Bharucha AE, Rao SSC, Shin AS. Surgical Interventions and the Use of Device-Aided Therapy for the Treatment of Fecal Incontinence and Defecatory Disorders. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017 Dec;15(12):1844-1854. doi: 10.1016/j.cgh.2017.08.023. Epub 2017 Aug 22. PMID: 28838787; PMCID: PMC5693715. Avaialble from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28838787/
